title: Game Theory
description: Model strategic interaction: dominance and the prisoner's dilemma, Nash equilibria, mixed strategies, zero-sum minimax, backward induction, and combinatorial winning strategies. Learn to ask not 'what's best for me?' but 'what's best for me given what everyone else will do?' — and to compute that equilibrium.
persona: For anyone who negotiates, competes, or cooperates — and for quant-interview preppers. No prerequisites beyond basic probability; every idea is grounded in a concrete game before it generalizes.

lessons.title: Dominance & the Prisoner's Dilemma
lessons.summary: A strictly dominant strategy beats every alternative no matter what others do — and mutual rationality can trap both players below the cooperative outcome.

lessons.title: Nash Equilibrium
lessons.summary: When no one has a dominant strategy, find a profile where everyone is best-responding at once — a cell no one wants to leave.

lessons.title: Mixed Strategies
lessons.summary: Some games have no pure equilibrium — you must randomize, mixing exactly so the opponent is indifferent.

lessons.title: Zero-Sum & Minimax
lessons.summary: In pure conflict every finite game has a value: the most you can guarantee equals the least your opponent can hold you to.

lessons.title: Sequential Games & Backward Induction
lessons.summary: When players move in turn, fold the game tree from the end — the logic behind the pirate split.

lessons.title: Winning Strategies: Nim & Symmetry
lessons.summary: In impartial take-away games, the nim-sum (XOR) and parity/symmetry tell you who wins — and the exact winning move.

tagline: Find equilibria where no one wants to deviate.
